     2|
     3|## Getting Started
     4|
     5|First, run the development server:
     6|
     7|```bash
     8|npm run dev
     9|# or
    10|yarn dev
    11|# or
    12|pnpm dev
    13|# or
    14|bun dev
    15|```
    16|
    17|Open [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000) with your browser to see the result.
    18|
    19|You can start editing the page by modifying `app/page.tsx`. The page auto-updates as you edit the file.
    20|
    21|This project uses [`next/font`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/fonts) to automatically optimize and load [Geist](https://vercel.com/font), a new font family for Vercel.
    22|
    23|## Learn More
    24|
    25|To learn more about Next.js, take a look at the following resources:
    26|
    27|- [Next.js Documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs) - learn about Next.js features and API.
    28|- [Learn Next.js](https://nextjs.org/learn) - an interactive Next.js tutorial.
    29|
    30|You can check out [the Next.js GitHub repository](https://github.com/vercel/next.js) - your feedback and contributions are welcome!
    31|
    32|## Deploy on Vercel
    33|
    34|The easiest way to deploy your Next.js app is to use the [Vercel Platform](https://vercel.com/new?utm_medium=default-template&filter=next.js&utm_source=create-next-app&utm_campaign=create-next-app-readme) from the creators of Next.js.

{"content": "     1|# Homesteader Labs - Project Context
     2|
     3|## Project Overview
     4|Homesteader Labs is a Next.js 14 application providing tools, hardware, and field documentation for homesteaders, survivalists, and self-reliant builders. The site features a "Brutalist" aesthetic with a focus on off-grid resilience, fabrication, and survival tech.
     5|
     6|### Core Technologies
     7|- **Framework:** Next.js 14 (App Router)
     8|- **Language:** TypeScript
     9|- **Styling:** Tailwind CSS (with Brutalist UI components)
    10|- **3D Rendering:** Three.js (via `STLViewer` for fabrication tools)
    11|- **Content:** MDX (for blog and archive posts)
    12|- **Icons:** Lucide React
    13|- **Testing:** Vitest with JSDOM
    14|
    15|### Key Features
    16|- **Planting Calendar:** Data-driven tool for managing crop schedules.
    17|- **Weather Tools:** Survival and planting-focused weather dashboards.
    18|- **Fabrication:** 3D STL viewer for hardware designs and field tools.
    19|- **Shop:** Catalog for off-grid hardware and survival gear.
    20|- **Terminal:** Interactive overlay (`ALT+T`) for command-line style interactions.
    21|
    22|---
    23|
    24|## Building and Running
    25|
    26|### Development
    27|```bash
    28|npm run dev
    29|```
    30|Starts the development server on `http://localhost:3000`.
    31|
    32|### Production
    33|```bash
    34|npm run build
    35|npm run start
    36|```
    37|Builds the application for production and starts the server.
    38|
    39|### Testing
    40|```bash
    41|npm run test
    42|```
    43|Runs the Vitest test suite.
    44|
    45|### Linting
    46|```bash
    47|npm run lint
    48|```
    49|Runs ESLint to check for code quality and style issues.
    50|
    51|---
    52|
    53|## Development Conventions
    54|
    55|### Architecture
    56|- **App Router:** All routing and layouts are under the `app/` directory.
    57|- **Modular Components:** Feature-specific components are in `components/<feature>/`, while shared UI elements are in `components/ui/`.
    58|- **Domain Logic:** All business logic, types, and utility functions are centralized in the `lib/` directory. Use the `@/lib` alias.
    59|- **Content Management:** MDX files are stored in `content/` and parsed using `lib/posts.ts` and `lib/products.ts`.
    60|
    61|### UI & Styling
    62|- **Brutalist Aesthetic:** Use standard UI components (`BrutalistBlock`, `Typography`, `Button`, `Badge`) to maintain the project's visual identity.
    63|- **Shadows & Borders:** Use the `shadow-brutalist` and `border-3` classes for consistency.
    64|- **Color Palette:** Strictly follow the CSS variables defined in `app/globals.css` and mapped in `tailwind.config.ts`.
    65|- **Dark Mode:** Supports `.dark` class toggling.
    66|
    67|### Performance
    68|- **Lazy Loading:** 3D components like `STLViewer` should be loaded dynamically using `next/dynamic` with `ssr: false` to keep the initial bundle size small.
    69|
    70|### Testing
    71|- **Unit Tests:** Place logic tests in `lib/` with a `.test.ts` extension (e.g., `lib/survivalIndex.test.ts`).
    72|- **UI Tests:** Use `@testing-library/react` for component testing within Vitest.
    73|
    74|---
    75|
    76|## Key Directories
    77|- `app/`: Next.js pages and API routes.
    78|- `components/`: React components.
    79|- `content/`: MDX content files.
    80|- `docs/`: Project documentation (Audits, MVP specs, Strategies).
    81|- `lib/`: Shared logic, types, and data fetching.
    82|- `public/`: Static assets (fonts, images, textures).
    83|", "total_lines": 82, "file_size": 3054, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"results": [{"task_index": 0, "status": "completed", "summary": "Here is my detailed analysis of all 10 files:

================================================================
FILE-BY-FILE ANALYSIS
================================================================

1. app/layout.tsx (40 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Root layout for the Next.js app. Sets up HTML shell, providers, navigation, footer, and visual effects.

QUALITY: Good. Clean and well-structured.

PATTERNS:
- Uses Next.js App Router metadata export (static)
- Readonly<> wrapper on children prop (good practice)
- Proper semantic HTML: <main> with role="main" and id
- Single Providers wrapper for all context

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- DUPLICATE METADATA: The title/description here is identical to page.tsx's metadata export. page.tsx metadata is redundant since layout.tsx already defines it. The page-level one overrides the layout-level one, but they're the same strings.
- NO FONT OPTIMIZATION: Uses font-mono from Tailwind classes but loads Caveat via a raw CSS @import in globals.css instead of using next/font. This causes a render-blocking request and FOUT (flash of unstyled text). Should use next/font/google.
- MISSING: No favicon or manifest in metadata. No viewport meta (though Next.js adds a default one).
- The body classes reference `bg-background-primary` and `text-foreground-primary` which map to CSS custom properties -- good bridge between Tailwind and CSS vars.

2. app/page.tsx (118 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Homepage. Hero section with CTA, featured products, tools showcase, archive posts, newsletter, and mission statement.

QUALITY: Good. Well-organized with clear section comments. Good use of component abstraction.

PATTERNS:
- Server Component (no "use client") -- correct since it has no interactivity
- Exports static metadata
- Uses custom UI components (Button, BrutalistBlock, Badge, Typography)
- Inline style for grid background pattern in hero

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- DUPLICATE METADATA: As noted, identical to layout.tsx. Remove from page.tsx or differentiate.
- INLINE STYLES: The grid background pattern (line 26) uses a complex inline style. Could be a Tailwind utility or a CSS class for reusability.
- ABSOLUTE POSITIONING: The background decoration div uses `absolute inset-0` which requires BrutalistBlock to have `position: relative` -- this works if BrutalistBlock sets that, but it's an implicit dependency.
- HARDCODED ROUTES: `/tools/planting-calendar/` and `/shop/` are hardcoded strings. A constants file for routes would improve maintainability.
- ACCESSIBILITY: The <kbd> elements for the terminal hint are good. The section structure is clean.

3. app/globals.css (200 of 266 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Global styles. CSS custom properties for theming, brutalist UI components, animations, accessibility, and custom scrollbar.

QUALITY: Solid. Good dark mode support, accessibility considerations.

PATTERNS:
- CSS custom properties for theming (--bg-primary, --text-primary, etc.)
- .dark class-based dark mode (matches Tailwind config)
- prefers-reduced-motion media query -- excellent accessibility
- Custom scrollbar styling
- ::selection styling

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- RENDER-BLOCKING FONT: Line 1 imports Google Fonts via @import url(). This is render-blocking. Should use next/font instead.
- TAILWIND CONFLICT: `.animate-pulse` (line 87-89) and `.backdrop-blur-sm` (line 77-79) are already Tailwind utilities. Redefining them may cause conflicts or unexpected behavior depending on CSS cascade order.
- GLOBAL SVG RULE: Line 97-100 sets `svg { display: inline-block; vertical-align: middle; }` globally. This affects ALL SVGs including third-party components and could cause layout issues.
- CANVAS POINTER-EVENTS: `canvas:not(.interactive) { pointer-events: none; }` is a reasonable workaround but fragile -- any new canvas (e.g., Three.js scenes) needs to remember the `.interactive` class.
- `image-rendering: crisp-edges` on body is unusual and may cause blurry images on non-retina screens or degrade photo quality. Should be scoped to specific elements.
- The .dymo-label and .field-station-box classes are well-themed for the brutalist aesthetic.
- HARDCODED DARK VALUES: `.dark .brutalist-block` uses `background-color: #000000` directly instead of a CSS variable, breaking the variable pattern.

4. tailwind.config.ts (51 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Tailwind CSS configuration. Extends colors, fonts, shadows, and animations.

QUALITY: Good. Clean, focused extension.

PATTERNS:
- CSS variable-based color system (bridges CSS vars to Tailwind utilities)
- Class-based dark mode
- Custom brutalist shadows at 3 sizes
- Custom animations for visual effects

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- BORDER COLOR CONFLICT: `border: { primary: 'var(--border-primary)' }` may conflict with Tailwind's default `border-primary` usage since Tailwind has its own `border` color namespace. Could cause `border-transparent`, `border-inherit`, etc. to break.
- MISSING KEYFRAMES: The config defines animations (condensation, scan, drip) but no corresponding keyframes. These must be defined in globals.css -- should verify they exist in the remaining 66 lines.
- NO PLUGINS: Empty plugins array is fine but could benefit from @tailwindcss/typography for MDX content styling.
- The `pages/` directory is in content paths but the app uses App Router, not Pages Router. Minor -- won't cause issues, just unnecessary.

5. next.config.mjs (21 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Next.js configuration with MDX support.

QUALITY: Minimal and correct.

PATTERNS:
- Uses @next/mdx for MDX processing
- remark-gfm for GitHub-flavored markdown
- Trailing slashes enabled
- Images unoptimized

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- IMAGES UNOPTIMIZED: `images: { unoptimized: true }` disables Next.js image optimization entirely. This is a significant performance concern for a production site. Usually set for static exports only -- if deploying to Vercel/Node, should be removed.
- NO OUTPUT SETTING: If this is meant for static export, it should have `output: 'export'`. If not, there's no reason for `unoptimized: true`.
- MDX REGEX: `extension: /\.mdx?$/` matches both .md and .mdx files. This is intentional but worth noting.
- NO SECURITY HEADERS: No headers config for CSP, HSTS, etc.
- NO REDIRECTS/REWRITES: Fine for now.

6. tsconfig.json (43 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: TypeScript configuration.

QUALITY: Standard Next.js 14 configuration. Correct.

PATTERNS:
- ES2017 target (good for broad browser support)
- Strict mode enabled (good)
- Bundler module resolution (correct for Next.js 14)
- Path alias @/* -> ./*
- Incremental compilation

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- `"dist/types/**/*.ts"` in include is unusual for a Next.js project. May be left over from a build process or library setup. Should verify if dist/ folder is needed.
- Could add `"forceConsistentCasingInImports": true` for cross-platform safety.
- Could add `"noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true` for stricter array access.
- Overall: no bugs, standard and correct.

7. vitest.config.ts (17 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Vitest test runner configuration.

QUALITY: Good. Minimal and correct.

PATTERNS:
- jsdom environment for React component testing
- Global test functions (describe, it, expect without imports)
- Path alias mirroring tsconfig
- Setup file reference

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- MISSING SETUP FILE: References `./vitest.setup.ts` -- should verify this file exists.
- Could add coverage configuration for CI.
- Could add `css: true` if testing styled components.
- The @vitejs/plugin-react is correctly used for JSX transform.

8. components/providers.tsx (14 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Client-side provider composition. Wraps children in FieldStation and Cart contexts.

QUALITY: Excellent. Clean, minimal, correct.

PATTERNS:
- "use client" directive (required because contexts use useState/useEffect)
- Simple composition of providers
- Correct nesting order (FieldStation outer, Cart inner)

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- IMPORT: Uses `React.ReactNode` without explicit React import. Works in React 18+ with the new JSX transform, but the React import is implicit.
- Could potentially add a ThemeProvider here if dark mode toggling is needed.
- Could add ErrorBoundary wrapping for resilience.
- Very clean -- no issues.

9. app/context/CartContext.tsx (141 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Shopping cart state management with localStorage persistence.

QUALITY: Very good. Well-structured with proper patterns.

PATTERNS:
- Context + Provider + custom hook pattern (canonical React)
- localStorage persistence with hydration guard (isLoaded flag)
- useCallback for stable function references
- Proper error handling on JSON parse
- Safe undefined check in useCart hook

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- INDENTATION BUG: Line 12 `interface CartContextType {` has extra leading spaces (2 spaces before `interface`). Minor but indicates inconsistent formatting.
- HYDRATION CONCERN: Items are rendered immediately (empty array) and then populated from localStorage in useEffect. This means the first render shows an empty cart on SSR-hydrated pages. The `isLoaded` flag is tracked but never exposed to consumers -- could lead to flash of empty cart. Should either expose isLoaded or conditionally render children.
- totalItems/totalPrice: These are computed on every render (lines 111-112). Could use useMemo for optimization, though with small cart sizes it's negligible.
- DEFAULT EXPORT: Exports both named `useCart` and default `CartContext`. The default export of the raw context is rarely needed and could be removed.
- NO SIZE LIMIT: No maximum cart size or quantity limit validation.

10. app/context/FieldStationContext.tsx (367 lines)
----------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Location management, frost date lookup, and growing zone estimation for the planting tools.

QUALITY: Good overall, but the most complex file with some notable issues.

PATTERNS:
- Same Context + Provider + Hook pattern as CartContext
- localStorage persistence with date object revival
- External API integration with fallback to mock data
- Zip-code-based growing zone estimation
- useCallback for stable references

ISSUES & IMPROVEMENTS:
- STALE CLOSURE BUG (CRITICAL): In `removeLocation` (line 195-205), `activeLocation` is in the dependency array but the function reads it inside a setState updater. The `activeLocation` reference could be stale. Should use a ref or read from the `prev` array to determine the active state.
- STALE CLOSURE BUG #2: In `lookupFrostDates` (line 228-337), `locations` is in the dependency array, causing the function to be recreated every time locations change. The inner `setLocations` callback (line 295) correctly uses the updater pattern, BUT the catch block (line 323) reads `locations` directly -- stale closure risk.
- SIDE EFFECTS IN setState: Lines 295-306 fire an async `geocodeZipCode` call inside a `setLocations` updater function. This is an anti-pattern -- setState updaters should be pure. The geocode call should happen outside the updater.
- DUPLICATE LOGIC: `getGrowingZoneFromZip` and `getMockFrostData` contain nearly identical zip-range-to-region mapping. Should be DRY'd with a single lookup table.
- UNUSED API CONSTANT: `FROST_API_BASE` is defined but the actual fetch URL is constructed inline. Good that it's extracted as a constant.
- DATE HANDLING: Date objects don't survive JSON.stringify/parse gracefully. The code manually revives them (lines 154-155) which is correct but fragile. A date serialization library or custom reviver would be more robust.
- LARGE FILE: At 367 lines, this context does a lot. Could be split into separate concerns: location management vs frost date lookup.
- switchLocation has a dead comment block (lines 221-224) suggesting auto-fetching frost dates on location switch -- incomplete feature.

================================================================
SUMMARY
================================================================

WHAT I DID:
- Read and analyzed all 10 requested files in detail.

WHAT I FOUND:

Overall Architecture: Well-structured Next.js 14 App Router project with a clean separation of concerns. Uses CSS custom properties bridged to Tailwind for theming. Two main contexts (Cart, FieldStation) handle client-side state with localStorage persistence.

Key Strengths:
- Clean component composition and provider pattern
- Good accessibility (prefers-reduced-motion, focus-visible, semantic HTML)
- Proper "use client" / server component boundaries
- Consistent brutalist/field-manual aesthetic in CSS
- localStorage hydration guard pattern (isLoaded flag)

Critical Issues:
1. STALE CLOSURE BUGS in FieldStationContext (removeLocation, lookupFrostDates catch block)
2. SIDE EFFECTS IN setState updaters in FieldStationContext
3. RENDER-BLOCKING FONT via CSS @import instead of next/font
4. IMAGES UNOPTIMIZED disables all Next.js image optimization

Moderate Issues:
5. Duplicate metadata in layout.tsx and page.tsx
6. Cart isLoaded not exposed to consumers (flash of empty state)
7. Tailwind utility conflicts from redefined classes in globals.css
8. Global SVG styling could break third-party components
9. Duplicate zip-to-region mapping logic in FieldStationContext
10. image-rendering: crisp-edges on body may degrade photo quality
11. Missing @tailwindcss/typography plugin for MDX content

=======================================================================
COMPONENT ANALYSIS REPORT — HOMESTEADER LABS (Next.js 14 / TypeScript)
=======================================================================

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
1. components/layout/Navigation.tsx (165 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Main site navigation with logo, desktop/mobile menus, dark mode toggle, low-FX toggle, and cart/requisition link.

CODE QUALITY: Good. Clean state management, proper "use client" directive, logical component structure.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Uses localStorage directly for persisting theme/FX prefs (no context/provider)
- Communicates with VisualEffects via custom DOM event ("hl-toggle-fx")
- Uses custom DymoLabel component for nav link styling

ACCESSIBILITY:
+ Good: aria-labels on icon-only buttons (dark mode, FX, menu toggle)
- Missing: no aria-expanded on mobile menu toggle button
- Missing: no role="navigation" or aria-label on the <nav> itself (though <nav> is semantic)
- Hidden text "HOMESTEADER_LABS" on small screens replaced by "HL_SYS" — fine

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- JSON.parse(savedDark) on line 20 could throw if localStorage has corrupted data — no try/catch
- localStorage access in useEffect is safe for SSR but if localStorage is disabled, it will throw
- Low FX toggle label says "ON/OFF" but the labeling is confusing: when lowFX=true, the button text reads "ON" (meaning low-FX mode is on, but UX-wise it's ambiguous)

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Wrap localStorage reads in try/catch
- Add aria-expanded={mobileMenuOpen} to the mobile toggle button
- Extract the dark mode / FX logic into a shared context or hook to avoid duplication between Navigation and Footer
- The event-bus pattern (window.dispatchEvent) is fragile; a React context would be more idiomatic

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
2. components/layout/Footer.tsx (83 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Site footer with brand info, navigation links, FX toggle, legal links.

CODE QUALITY: Clean and compact. Consistent with the brutalist aesthetic.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Duplicates FX toggle logic from Navigation (localStorage + custom event dispatch)
- Decorative background text via absolute positioning

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Buttons and links are text-based and visible
- Very low opacity values (opacity-30, opacity-20) may fail WCAG contrast requirements
- The decorative text at opacity 0.02 is correctly marked pointer-events-none/select-none

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- Same localStorage vulnerability as Navigation (no try/catch)
- Footer FX state is loaded once on mount but never listens for the "hl-toggle-fx" event, so if Navigation toggles FX, Footer's button won't update to reflect the change

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Add an event listener (like VisualEffects does) to sync FX state
- Extract shared FX toggle logic into a hook (e.g., useLowFX())
- Improve contrast ratios for very low-opacity text for accessibility compliance

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
3. components/layout/VisualEffects.tsx (50 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Renders three fixed overlay layers (grain/noise SVG, concrete texture image, CRT scanlines) that contribute to the brutalist aesthetic. Toggleable via low-FX mode.

CODE QUALITY: Simple and effective. Clean event-driven toggling.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Listens for custom DOM event "hl-toggle-fx" to toggle visibility
- z-index 9999/9998 for overlays, z-[-1] for texture — ensures proper stacking

ACCESSIBILITY:
+ All layers have pointer-events-none so they don't interfere with interaction
- CRT scanlines at z-[9998] with very low opacity — low risk but technically overlays full page

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- The texture layer at z-[-1] might not render on all stacking contexts
- Relies on an external image ('/textures/subtle-concrete-mildev.jpg') — if missing, the layer silently fails
- SVG noise uses an inline data URI — functional but hard to maintain

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Add a CSS class toggle on <html> or <body> instead of conditionally rendering, which would be more performant (no DOM insertion/removal)
- Consider using CSS-only noise via a utility class
- The mix-blend-multiply on the texture layer won't work in dark mode as expected (mixing with dark backgrounds produces nearly invisible results)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
4. components/terminal/TerminalOverlay.tsx (362 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Full terminal emulator overlay with command execution, boot sequence, editor mode, keyboard shortcuts (Alt+T, Escape).

CODE QUALITY: Good overall. Well-structured command registry pattern. Boot sequence simulation is creative.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Command registry as a static Record<string, (args) => string[]>
- Boot sequence with randomized delays
- Editor mode with Save/Cancel
- Uses multiple refs (input focus, terminal scroll, boot state)

ACCESSIBILITY:
+ Close button has aria-label
- Terminal input has no aria-label or role
- The overlay has no role="dialog" or aria-modal="true"
- No focus trapping — user can tab out of the terminal overlay
- Screen readers won't understand the terminal output structure

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- Boot sequence uses setTimeout without cleanup — if component unmounts during boot, state updates occur on unmounted component
- "fx" command accesses localStorage directly inside a static function — won't catch errors
- "edit" command doesn't add an entry to commands history (no output shown)
- Editor ESC shortcut mentioned in header text but not actually implemented — only ESC-to-close-terminal works

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Add role="dialog" aria-modal="true" to the overlay
- Add focus trapping
- Clean up boot timeouts on unmount
- Consider command history (up/down arrow)
- The editor mode is a fun concept but "saving" does nothing persistent — could be confusing to users
- Add aria-live="polite" to terminal output area for screen reader announcements

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
5. components/home/FeaturedProducts.tsx (64 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Displays first 3 products in a grid on the homepage.

CODE QUALITY: Clean, no "use client" directive — this is a server component. Calls getAllProducts() at render time.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Server component fetching data via lib function
- Uses CSS class "brutalist-block" directly (instead of the BrutalistBlock component) — inconsistent with other components
- Uses "dymo-label" CSS class directly instead of DymoLabel component

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Product cards are entire links — good for click targets
- The price uses a $ prefix which could be confusing for screen readers (concatenated with template literal)

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- Uses inline CSS variables via `text-[var(--accent)]` instead of the Tailwind theme tokens used elsewhere — inconsistent
- product.id.toLowerCase() in href — if products have IDs with special chars, this could break

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Use BrutalistBlock component instead of raw CSS class for consistency
- Use DymoLabel component instead of raw CSS class
- Use Tailwind color tokens (text-accent) instead of var(--accent) for consistency
- Add alt text or aria-labels for the product "image" placeholders

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
6. components/home/ToolsShowcase.tsx (84 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Displays available tools (Fabrication, Weather) in a 2-column grid.

CODE QUALITY: Good. Uses BrutalistBlock and Typography components properly.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Icon mapping via object lookup (IconMap)
- Server component (no "use client")
- Hardcoded tool data — appropriate for a small static list

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Links wrap entire cards — good target size
- "Access Tool" text appears only on hover (opacity-0 to opacity-100) — invisible to keyboard users without hover

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- The `as keyof typeof IconMap` cast is safe but if a new tool.icon string doesn't match, ToolIcon would be undefined, causing a runtime crash
- Uses var(--accent) inconsistently with other components that use text-accent

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Add a fallback icon for unknown icon strings
- Make "Access Tool" text visible (or use aria-label) for keyboard/screen reader users
- Use Tailwind theme tokens consistently

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
7. components/home/NewsletterSignup.tsx (85 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Email signup form with mock submission logic.

CODE QUALITY: Decent. Simple form with status feedback.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- "use client" for form state
- Simple email validation (just checks for "@" — intentionally minimal)
- Auto-clears success status after 3s

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Input has type="email" and placeholder — but NO <label> element
- Status messages have no aria-live region — screen readers won't announce changes
- Error/success messages are color-coded only (no icon or text prefix besides the ">>" which helps)

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- The CSS class `bg-[var(--accent)]/10` on BrutalistBlock — the Tailwind opacity modifier on a CSS variable might not work correctly depending on the format of --accent. If --accent is a hex value, `/10` won't apply opacity.
- The setTimeout for resetting success status runs without cleanup — if the component unmounts, it triggers a state update

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Add a proper <label> for the email input (visually hidden if needed)
- Add aria-live="polite" to status message area

- Clean up setTimeout on unmount
- Fix the CSS variable with opacity issue

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
8. components/home/RecentArchivePosts.tsx (84 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Shows latest 3 blog/archive posts on the homepage.

CODE QUALITY: Good. Clean server component structure.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Server component, data from getAllPosts()
- Uses raw "brutalist-block" and "dymo-label" CSS classes (like FeaturedProducts, not using the React components)
- Empty state handling at the bottom

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Entire post rows are links — good
- Tags have very low opacity (opacity-40) — potential contrast issues
- Good use of truncation with line-clamp-2

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- post.excerpt || post.description — if both are undefined, renders nothing (no fallback)
- Empty state check at line 77 appears after the map — if posts is empty, the space-y-4 div still renders empty

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Use BrutalistBlock/DymoLabel components for consistency

- Add aria-labels describing each post link
- Use Tailwind tokens consistently

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
9. components/ui/BrutalistBlock.tsx (49 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Core UI primitive — a styled container block with variant support, optional title and ref tag.

CODE QUALITY: Good. Clean, small, well-typed.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Supports polymorphic rendering via `as` prop (defaults to 'div')
- Three variants: default, accent, terminal
- The refTag feature adds a small label at the bottom — nice design touch

ACCESSIBILITY:
- The refTag uses absolute positioning with z-20 — could overlap with interactive elements
- Title uses h3 hardcoded — this could break heading hierarchy

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- The `as` prop is typed as React.ElementType but it's not validated, meaning any element could be passed, potentially breaking styles

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Consider using forwardRef for ref forwarding
- Allow title heading level to be configurable
- Add an optional id prop for section linking

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
10. components/ui/Typography.tsx (37 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Reusable typography component with variant-based styling.

CODE QUALITY: Compact but slightly over-clever in the Component derivation logic.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Auto-derives the HTML element from the variant (h1->h1, body->p, small->span, code->span)
- Allows explicit override via `as` prop

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Good semantic element mapping
- No ARIA roles needed — correct HTML elements are used

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- Line 18: `variant as React.ElementType` cast — the 'code' variant maps to a 'span' element, but if someone adds a variant like 'label', the ternary won't handle it
- The 'code' variant renders as a <span>, not a <code> element — misleading name

IMPROVEMENTS:
- The element derivation logic should be explicit (a mapping object) rather than inference
- 'code' variant should render as <code> element by default
- Consider adding a 'label' or 'caption' variant
- Support the `id` prop for heading anchors

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
11. components/ui/Button.tsx (52 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Reusable button/link component with variant and size props.

CODE QUALITY: Good. Supports both button and Link rendering.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Dual-mode: renders <Link> when href is provided, <button> otherwise

- Four variants and three sizes

+ Good: disabled styling included

POTENTIAL BUGS:

- className prop is listed in the interface AND comes from ButtonHTMLAttributes — double declaration, though not a runtime issue

IMPROVEMENTS:

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
12. components/ui/Badge.tsx (34 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Small label/badge component with outline/solid/status variants and optional pulse indicator.

CODE QUALITY: Very clean and simple.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Renders as a <div> containing an optional pulse dot and a <span>
- Three variants for different visual treatments

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- Renders as <div> — if used inline (e.g., in navigation), this creates invalid HTML (div inside anchor/span)
- Pulse dot is always green regardless of variant/context — could be misleading

IMPROVEMENTS:

- Add optional role="status" for status badges

13. components/ui/DymoLabel.tsx (16 lines)

- Make the styling self-contained (Tailwind classes) rather than relying on global CSS
- Add a `as` prop for element polymorphism (so it can render as <span> or <label>)
- Some usages in Navigation use DymoLabel, but FeaturedProducts and RecentArchivePosts use the raw CSS class — inconsistency in the codebase

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
14. components/ui/FieldStationLayout.tsx (84 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Page-level layout wrapper with decorative grid background, corner brackets, metadata header/footer, and coordinate display.

CODE QUALITY: Good. Well-structured with clear visual hierarchy.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- "use client" because it consumes FieldStationContext
- Decorative corner brackets via border tricks
- Grid lines via CSS background-image gradients
- Pulls lat/lon from FieldStationContext with hardcoded fallbacks

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Decorative elements properly have pointer-events-none
- The "Active_Transmission_Link..." animate-pulse text is purely decorative but has no aria-hidden

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- Uses var(--text-primary) in inline CSS but other components use Tailwind token names — if the CSS variable name changes, this breaks silently
- The footer metadata section has animate-pulse which could be distracting/inaccessible for users with motion sensitivity (prefers-reduced-motion not respected)

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Respect prefers-reduced-motion for the animate-pulse
- Add aria-hidden="true" to purely decorative elements
- Consider making the stationId dynamic from context rather than a prop
- Use consistent CSS variable naming

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
15. components/ui/Marginalia.tsx (24 lines)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
PURPOSE: Decorative marginal note component with configurable rotation.

CODE QUALITY: Minimal and clean.

DESIGN PATTERNS:
- Applies rotation via inline style
- Relies on global "marginalia" CSS class for positioning/styling

ACCESSIBILITY:
- Rotated text can be harder to read
- No aria-hidden — if this is decorative, it should be marked as such

POTENTIAL BUGS:
- If "marginalia" CSS class isn't defined, renders unstyled
- Rotation applied via inline style will override any CSS transform animations

IMPROVEMENTS:
- Self-contain styling in Tailwind classes
- Add aria-hidden="true" option for decorative usage
- Allow positioning props (side: 'left' | 'right')

=======================================================================
CROSS-CUTTING FINDINGS
=======================================================================

1. STATE MANAGEMENT FRAGMENTATION:
   - Dark mode and Low-FX state managed via localStorage + custom DOM events
   - Three components independently read/write the same localStorage keys
   - Should be consolidated into a React Context or Zustand store

2. INCONSISTENT COMPONENT USAGE:
   - FeaturedProducts and RecentArchivePosts use raw CSS classes
     ("brutalist-block", "dymo-label") instead of the React component wrappers
     (BrutalistBlock, DymoLabel). Should pick one approach.

3. CSS VARIABLE vs TAILWIND TOKEN INCONSISTENCY:
   - Some components use text-[var(--accent)], others use text-accent,
     and FieldStationLayout uses var(--text-primary) in inline styles.
   - Should standardize on Tailwind tokens.

4. ACCESSIBILITY GAPS:
   - No focus-visible ring styles across interactive elements
   - Missing aria-expanded on mobile menu
   - Missing role="dialog" + aria-modal on terminal overlay
   - Missing <label> on newsletter email input
   - Missing aria-live regions for dynamic status updates
   - Very low opacity text (0.02-0.4) likely fails WCAG contrast

5. ERROR HANDLING:
   - No try/catch around localStorage operations (will throw if disabled)
   - No fallback icons in ToolsShowcase if icon key is invalid

6. MINOR BUGS:
   - Badge and DymoLabel render as <div> but are used inline
   - Typography 'code' variant renders <span> instead of <code>
   - setTimeout in NewsletterSignup not cleaned up on unmount
   - Terminal boot sequence timeouts not cleaned up on unmount
   - Footer FX toggle doesn't listen for external changes

=======================================================================
SUMMARY
=======================================================================
What I did: Read and analyzed all 15 component files in detail.

What I found: A well-crafted brutalist-themed UI system with creative design 
(terminal overlay, DymoLabels, field station layouts, CRT effects). The code 
is generally clean TypeScript with good component decomposition. However, 
there are systemic issues around: (a) fragmented state management for 
theme/FX preferences using localStorage + DOM events instead of React 
context, (b) inconsistent use of UI wrapper components vs raw CSS classes, 
(c) CSS variable usage inconsistency, (d) several accessibility gaps 
(missing ARIA attributes, contrast issues, no focus management), and 
(e) minor memory leak potential from uncleaned timeouts.

Files created or modified: None — this was a read-only analysis.

1. lib/posts.ts

Quality: GOOD. Clean MDX blog post loader using gray-matter.

  * Date sorting uses string comparison which works for ISO dates but is fragile
  * No caching - every call re-reads all files from disk
- Improvements: Add a memoization layer; combine tag/category extraction with post loading

2. lib/products.ts
------------------
Quality: GOOD but minimal. Hardcoded product data with 2 items.
- Exports: Product interface, products array, getAllProducts(), getProductBySlug(), getProductsByCategory()
- Pattern: Static in-memory product catalog

3. lib/weatherApi.ts (652 lines)
---------------------------------
Quality: VERY GOOD. Comprehensive weather API integration layer.
- Uses Open-Meteo API (free, no key needed), NWS API for alerts, Zippopotam for zip codes
- In-memory cache with 5-min TTL, coordinate rounding to 2 decimals for cache keys
- Features: weather fetch, geocoding (city + zip), air quality, historical comparison, moon phase calculation, dew point calculation
- Potential issues:
  * getMoonPhase() `daysUntilFull` calculation has a unit bug on line 623: divides by milliseconds after already working in days. `lunarAge` is already in days (line 611-612 correction: actually lunarAge ends up being `ms / ms` from line 611 divided by ms on 612 but `synodicMonth` is in ms on line 609). Actually wait - line 611: `daysSinceNewMoon` is in days. Line 612: `lunarAge = daysSinceNewMoon % synodicMonth` but synodicMonth is in MILLISECONDS (line 609). This is a BUG - mixing days and milliseconds in the modulo operation. The normalizedAge will be wrong.
  * Actually re-reading: line 611 divides by `(24*60*60*1000)` so daysSinceNewMoon is in days. Line 609 synodicMonth is in ms. So `lunarAge = daysSinceNewMoon_in_days % synodicMonth_in_ms` is broken. The normalizedAge would be essentially `daysSinceNewMoon / synodicMonth_ms` which would be a tiny number. However the code does work at runtime (no TS errors) and the moon display exists - so this might produce inaccurate phases.
  * Line 613: `normalizedAge = (lunarAge / synodicMonth) * 8` - since both are in different units, this won't give correct phases.
  * FIX: synodicMonth should be in days (29.530588853) not milliseconds, OR daysSinceNewMoon should stay in ms.
  * fetchHistoricalFrostData() is a stub returning null
  * In-memory cache won't persist across serverless function invocations
  * No rate limiting on NWS API calls
- Improvements: Fix moon phase unit mismatch; consider edge caching; implement frost data

Here's my full review of the Homesteader Labs project:

========================================================================
PROJECT REVIEW: HOMESTEADER LABS
========================================================================

Stack: Next.js 14 (App Router) / TypeScript / Tailwind / Three.js / MDX
Status: TypeScript compiles clean. ESLint passes clean. Well-structured.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
WHAT'S WORKING WELL
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  1. Architecture is solid. Clean separation: pages in app/, components
     split by feature, business logic centralized in lib/, types well-
     defined. The App Router usage is correct (server vs client boundaries).

  2. The brutalist UI system is cohesive and creative. BrutalistBlock,
     DymoLabel, FieldStationLayout, TerminalOverlay, CRT scanlines --
     it all hangs together really well as a visual identity.

  3. Weather tool is the most mature feature -- in-memory caching with
     TTL, exponential retry backoff, NWS alerts integration, survival
     and planting dashboards, data export (JSON/CSV). Nice work.

  4. Planting calendar is impressive -- 22-crop database with succession
     planting, lunar phase sync, season extension modifiers (row cover,
     cold frame, greenhouse), and contextual hardware cross-sell.

  5. Good testing setup -- Vitest with JSDOM, tests for planting
     calculations, survival index, and field station context.

  6. Cart system is well-done -- Context + localStorage persistence with
     proper hydration guards.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
BUGS (should fix)
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  BUG 1 - STLViewer volume calculation is wrong
    File: components/fabrication/STLViewer.tsx
    Issue: signedVolumeOfTriangle() calls p2.cross(p3), which MUTATES p2
    in Three.js (Vector3.cross() modifies in-place). This corrupts
    subsequent vertex calculations.
    Fix: Use new THREE.Vector3().crossVectors(p2, p3) instead.

  BUG 2 - Moon phase calculation has unit mismatch
    File: lib/weatherApi.ts (~line 609-613)
    Issue: synodicMonth is in milliseconds but daysSinceNewMoon is in
    days. The modulo and normalization math produces wrong phases.
    Fix: Convert synodicMonth to days (29.530588853) or keep everything
    in milliseconds.

  BUG 3 - Fabrication filament switching uses stale closure
    File: app/tools/fabrication/page.tsx (~line 109)
    Issue: updateFilament() back-calculates volume using the OLD filament
    density instead of the new one, producing incorrect cost estimates.
    Fix: Store raw volume separately instead of deriving it from weight.

  BUG 4 - Stale closures in FieldStationContext
    File: app/context/FieldStationContext.tsx
    Issue: removeLocation reads potentially stale activeLocation.
    lookupFrostDates catch block reads stale locations. Also, async
    geocodeZipCode is called inside a setState updater (anti-pattern).

------------------------------------------------------------------------
MODERATE ISSUES
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  * RENDER-BLOCKING FONT: globals.css line 1 uses @import url() for
    Google Fonts. Should use next/font/google instead. Causes FOUT.

  * IMAGES UNOPTIMIZED: next.config.mjs disables all image optimization.
    If you're deploying to Vercel/Node (not static export), this is a
    big perf hit. Remove unoptimized: true.

  * FRAGMENTED STATE MANAGEMENT: Dark mode and low-FX preferences are
    managed via raw localStorage + custom DOM events across Navigation,
    Footer, and VisualEffects independently. This leads to sync issues
    (Footer FX toggle doesn't update when Navigation toggles it).
    Consolidate into a React context or a shared hook.

  * BLOG IS A STUB: app/blog/ pages are hardcoded placeholders with no
    actual content loading. app/archive/ is the functional content system
    using MDX. Either wire blog/ to real content or redirect to archive/.

  * INCONSISTENT COMPONENT USAGE: FeaturedProducts and RecentArchivePosts
    use raw CSS classes ("brutalist-block", "dymo-label") while other
    components use the React wrappers (BrutalistBlock, DymoLabel). Pick
    one approach.

  * CSS VARIABLE INCONSISTENCY: Some files use text-[var(--accent)],
    others use text-accent (Tailwind token), and FieldStationLayout uses
    var(--text-primary) in inline styles. Standardize on Tailwind tokens.

  * NO localStorage ERROR HANDLING: Navigation and Footer read
    localStorage without try/catch. Throws if storage is disabled.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
ACCESSIBILITY GAPS
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  - TerminalOverlay: missing role="dialog", aria-modal, focus trapping
  - Navigation: missing aria-expanded on mobile menu toggle
  - NewsletterSignup: missing <label> on email input, no aria-live
  - FieldStationLayout: animate-pulse ignores prefers-reduced-motion
  - Badge renders as <div> but used inline (invalid HTML nesting)
  - Typography 'code' variant renders <span> instead of <code>
  - Very low opacity text (0.02-0.4) likely fails WCAG contrast
  - ToolsShowcase "Access Tool" text only visible on hover (hidden from
    keyboard users)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
MINOR / NICE-TO-HAVES
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  - Several setTimeout calls (TerminalOverlay boot, NewsletterSignup
    success reset) lack cleanup on unmount -- potential memory leaks
  - archive/page.tsx renders tag filter UI but it's non-functional
    (server component, no state for filtering)
  - lib/posts.ts: getAllTags() and getAllCategories() both call
    getAllPosts() internally -- inefficient double file reads
  - fabricationTypes.ts: supportEnabled and wallCount are defined but
    not factored into cost calculations
  - STLViewer has console.log statements left in
  - Consider @tailwindcss/typography plugin for MDX content styling
  - No security headers configured (CSP, HSTS, etc.)
  - README is still the default create-next-app template -- GEMINI.md
    is the real docs (nice and thorough btw)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
OVERALL ASSESSMENT
------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is a well-built project with a strong identity. The brutalist
aesthetic is executed consistently, the domain logic (planting
calculations, survival indices, weather integration) is thoughtful,
and the codebase is cleanly organized.

The 3 actual bugs (STL volume mutation, moon phase units, filament
stale closure) should be fixed since they produce incorrect results
for users. The state management fragmentation and accessibility gaps
are the next priority tier.

For a v0.1, this is solid. Nice work.

========================================================================
TODO LIST - HOMESTEADER LABS (23 items)
========================================================================

--- CRITICAL: Bugs producing wrong results for users ---

 1. [ ] STLViewer volume calculation - p2.cross(p3) mutates vector
 2. [ ] Moon phase unit mismatch (days vs milliseconds)
 3. [ ] Fabrication filament switch stale closure (wrong cost estimates)
 4. [ ] FieldStationContext stale closures + setState side effects

--- HIGH: Performance ---

 5. [ ] Replace CSS @import Google Font with next/font (render-blocking)
 6. [ ] Re-enable Next.js image optimization (if not static export)

--- HIGH: Architecture ---

 7. [ ] Consolidate dark mode + low-FX into React context (sync issues)

--- MEDIUM: Accessibility ---

 8. [ ] TerminalOverlay: role=dialog, aria-modal, focus trap
 9. [ ] Navigation: aria-expanded on mobile menu toggle
10. [ ] NewsletterSignup: <label> + aria-live for status
11. [ ] Badge as <span>, Typography code variant, reduced-motion, etc.

--- MEDIUM: Content & Features ---

12. [ ] Wire blog/ to real content or redirect to archive/
13. [ ] Archive page tag filter is non-functional (needs state)

--- MEDIUM: Code Quality ---

14. [ ] Standardize on BrutalistBlock/DymoLabel components (not raw CSS)
15. [ ] Standardize CSS tokens (Tailwind tokens everywhere)
16. [ ] Wrap localStorage reads in try/catch
17. [ ] Add useEffect cleanup for setTimeout calls (memory leaks)
18. [ ] Implement supportEnabled/wallCount in fabrication cost calc

--- LOW: Cleanup ---

19. [ ] Remove console.log from STLViewer.tsx
20. [ ] Memoize lib/posts.ts (double file reads for tags/categories)

--- LOW: Nice-to-haves ---

21. [ ] Replace default README with real project docs
22. [ ] Add security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
23. [ ] Add @tailwindcss/typography plugin for MDX styling

========================================================================

  [x] BUG 1 - STLViewer volume calculation
      File: components/fabrication/STLViewer.tsx
      Fix:  Replaced p2.cross(p3) (which mutates p2) with
            new Vector3().crossVectors(p2, p3)

  [x] BUG 2 - Moon phase unit mismatch
      File: lib/weatherApi.ts
      Fix:  Changed synodicMonth from milliseconds to days (29.530588853).
            Removed spurious ms-to-days divisions in daysUntilFull and
            daysUntilNew calculations.

  [x] BUG 3 - Fabrication filament stale closure
      File: app/tools/fabrication/page.tsx
      Fix:  Added rawVolumeRef to store the original volume from STL
            parsing. updateSettings() and updateFilament() now use this
            ref instead of back-calculating volume from weight/density.

  [x] BUG 4 - FieldStationContext stale closures
      File: app/context/FieldStationContext.tsx
      Fix:  - removeLocation now uses setActiveLocation updater pattern
              instead of reading stale activeLocation closure
            - Removed `locations` from lookupFrostDates dependencies
            - Moved geocodeZipCode calls out of setState updaters
            - Catch block now uses setLocations updater to check
              current locations instead of stale closure

Yeah I can see the problem clearly. Two issues:

1. **Caveat** (the handwriting font) in marginalia -- not great for legibility
2. **Tons of micro text** -- there's text-[7px], text-[8px], text-[9px], text-[10px] all over the place. Combined with Courier New monospace (which renders narrow), that's genuinely hard to read, especially on mobile

Let me audit exactly how bad it is and fix it all at once.
messages.reasoning:
Now I can see the full picture. There are two main issues:

1. **Caveat handwriting font** used in `.marginalia` - it's a cursive font that's hard to read
2. **Extremely small font sizes everywhere** - tons of `text-[7px]`, `text-[8px]`, `text-[9px]`, `text-[10px]` scattered across the entire codebase. Combined with Courier New mono (which renders narrow and small), this creates serious legibility issues especially on mobile.

The base body font is Courier New monospace. The Typography component's `small` variant is `text-sm` (14px) which is okay, but the custom pixel sizes go way too small.


Looking at the Typography variants, the heading and body scales seem reasonable on their own—body text starts at 16px and scales up to 18px on medium screens. The real problem is that Courier New inherently renders narrower and smaller than proportional fonts at the same size, so those tiny custom pixel sizes become nearly unreadable.

I think the best approach is to avoid touching every component individually. Instead, I can use a CSS media query to enforce a minimum font size on mobile, bump up the base font size slightly, and leverage Tailwind's configuration to make these changes systematically rather than creating a massive diff across the codebase. I'm mapping out the specific font-size increases needed across the codebase—bumping the tiny pixel values up by 2-3px each and making sure the body base size is explicit at 16px for desktop with mobile adjustments, while also addressing the Caveat font readability issue.

{"code": "from hermes_tools import search_files
import re
from collections import Counter

# Count all the tiny text sizes across tsx files
result = search_files(r'text-\[\d+px\]', target='content', path='.', file_glob='*.tsx', limit=200)
sizes = Counter()
for m in result.get('matches', []):
    found = re.findall(r'text-\[(\d+)px\]', m['content'])
    for s in found:
        sizes[int(s)] += 1

print("Tiny text size usage across .tsx files:")
for size in sorted(sizes.keys()):
    print(f"  text-[{size}px]: {sizes[size]} occurrences")
print(f"\n  Total micro-text instances: {sum(sizes.values())}")
"}

messages.reasoning:
That's 182 instances of micro text (excluding the 60px decorative one and the 4 instances of 11px). Let me plan the size bumps:

- text-[7px] -> text-[10px] (4 occurrences) - was unreadable, now tiny-but-readable
- text-[8px] -> text-[11px] (45 occurrences) - +3px
- text-[9px] -> text-[11px] (44 occurrences) - +2px, merge with 8px tier
- text-[10px] -> text-xs (89 occurrences) - text-xs = 12px in Tailwind


- text-[10px] -> text-xs (89 occurrences) - text-xs = 12px in Tailwind

Though I need to be careful here—some of these tiny sizes might be intentionally decorative. The user asked for a couple points bump, so I'll aim for +2-3px across the board while setting sensible minimums. I'll stick with Tailwind's standard sizes where I can for consistency: 7px becomes 10px, 8px becomes 11px, 9px becomes 12px (text-xs), and 10px stays at 12px or bumps to 13px. I'm realizing that collapsing everything to the same size defeats the purpose, so I need to preserve some visual hierarchy while still improving legibility. My revised approach bumps each tier by 2-3 pixels—mapping 7px to 9px, 8px to 10px, 9px to 11px, and 10px to the text-xs token at 12px—which gives me three distinct sizes that are all readable. I should also check if the body text and Typography small variant need similar adjustments on mobile.

{"code": "from hermes_tools import search_files, patch
import os

# Get all tsx files that contain tiny text sizes
result = search_files(r'text-\[(7|8|9|10)px\]', target='content', path='.', file_glob='*.tsx', output_mode='files_only', limit=100)
files = [m['path'] for m in result.get('matches', [])]
# Deduplicate
files = sorted(set(files))

print(f"Files to update: {len(files)}")
for f in files:
    print(f"  {f}")

# Apply replacements in order: most specific first to avoid double-replacing
# Do 10px first (won't conflict), then 9px, 8px, 7px
replacements = [
    ('text-[10px]', 'text-xs'),      # 10px -> 12px (Tailwind text-xs)
    ('text-[9px]', 'text-[11px]'),   # 9px -> 11px
    ('text-[8px]', 'text-[10px]'),   # 8px -> 10px
    ('text-[7px]', 'text-[9px]'),    # 7px -> 9px
]

for old, new in replacements:
    for filepath in files:
        try:
            r = patch(filepath, old, new, replace_all=True)
            if r.get('success'):
                # Count changes from diff
                diff = r.get('diff', '')
                changes = diff.count(f'+')  # rough count
                if changes > 0:
                    print(f"  {filepath}: {old} -> {new}")
        except Exception as e:
            # Might not have that particular size in this file
            pass

print("\nDone with bulk size replacements.")
"}

Done. Here's what I changed:

========================================================================
FONT READABILITY FIX
========================================================================

The problem was twofold:

  1. MICRO TEXT EVERYWHERE: 182 instances of text between 7-10px
     scattered across 37 files, plus the monospace font (Courier New)
     renders narrower than proportional fonts at the same size.

  2. CAVEAT HANDWRITING FONT: Hard to read at small sizes, and the
     marginalia overlaps content on mobile.

What I did:

  SIZE BUMPS (+2px, preserving visual hierarchy):
    7px  -> 9px    (4 instances)
    8px  -> 10px   (45 instances)
    9px  -> 11px   (44 instances)
    10px -> 12px   (89 instances, now using Tailwind text-xs)

  CSS FIXES:
    - Body: 16px base, 17px on mobile for monospace legibility
    - Marginalia: 1.1rem -> 1.3rem, bold weight, better line-height,
      HIDDEN entirely on mobile (prevents content overlap)
    - Dymo labels: 12px -> 13px
    - Typography small: 13px mobile, 14px desktop

  The look is the same -- just everything's ~2px bigger,
  so you shouldn't need to squint anymore.

{"content": "     1|# STRATEGY: CALORIC SECURITY & SURVIVAL INVENTORY SYSTEM
     2|
     3|## 1. Overview
     4|This document outlines the strategic integration of the **Planting Calendar** with a **Survival Inventory System**. The goal is to move from simple date calculations to a comprehensive "Caloric Security" dashboard that helps homesteaders and survivalists quantify their self-reliance.
     5|
     6|## 2. Core Concepts
     7|The system will bridge the gap between **Production** (Growing/Catching) and **Consumption** (Survival Needs).
     8|
     9|### A. The "Closed Loop" Architecture
    10|- **Production (Inputs):** Seeded quantities, roof catchment area (sq ft), storage capacity (gallons), solar array capacity.
    11|- **Environmental (Modifiers):** Real-time weather data (precipitation probability), evaporation rates, frost dates, lunar phases.
    12|- **Analytics (Logic):** Yield-to-calorie conversion, runoff efficiency coefficients, battery discharge curves.
    13|- **Security (Outputs):** "Days of Autonomy" (How many days can this homestead survive on current/projected resources?).
    14|
    15|---
    16|
    17|## 3. Technical Implementation Plan
    18|
    19|### Phase 1: Data Augmentation (Static)
    20|... (rest of Phase 1)
    21|
    22|### Phase 2: User Configuration & Persistence
    23|Update `FieldStationContext.tsx` to include a `HomesteadConfig` and `SurvivalManifest`.
    24|- **Water Catchment Config:**
    25|  ```typescript
    26|  interface CatchmentConfig {
    27|    roofAreaSqFt: number;      // Footprint of the collection surface
    28|    collectionEfficiency: number; // Coefficient (0.75 - 0.95) based on material (metal vs shingle)
    29|    firstFlushDivert: number;  // Gallons diverted per rain event for filtration
    30|    totalStorageCapacity: number; // Size of cistern/tank array
    31|  }
    32|  ```
    33|- **Storage:** Use `localStorage` to persist user data between sessions.
    34|- **Data Structure:**
    35|  ```typescript
    36|  interface InventoryItem {
    37|    id: string;
    38|    type: 'crop' | 'water' | 'fuel';
    39|    quantity: number;      // e.g., 24 plants, 500 gallons current
    40|    status: 'planned' | 'active' | 'stored';
    41|    lastUpdated: Date;
    42|  }
    43|  ```
    44|
    45|### Phase 3: The "Autonomy Dashboard"
    46|A new UI module within the `SurvivalDashboard` that translates raw inventory and catchment potential into survival metrics.
    47|- **Hydration Security:** `(Current Stored + (Forecasted Rain * Area * Efficiency)) / (Household Size * 1 Gallon) = Days of Water`.
    48|- **Caloric Security:** `(Total Plants * Yield * Calories) / (Household Size * 2000 kcal) = Days of Food`.
    49|- **Energy Security:** `(Battery Capacity + Solar Forecast) / (Baseload Watts) = Hours of Power`.
    50|
    51|---
    52|
    53|## 4. Integration with Weather Telemetry
    54|The existing `lib/survivalIndex.ts` will act as a real-time "Modifier" for the inventory:
    55|- **Drought Warning:** If `waterCatchment` score is < 20 for 14 days, flag "Crop Failure Risk" and reduce projected yields by 30%.
    56|- **Frost Alert:** If a freeze is detected before `harvestDate`, trigger a terminal alert to "Protect Inventory."
    57|- **Solar Surplus:** If `solarEfficiency` is > 90%, suggest "Dehydration/Canning Day" to utilize excess power for food preservation.
    58|
    59|---
    60|
    61|## 5. Future Expansion (The "Prepper" Niche)
    62|- **Macro-Nutrient Tracking:** Breakdown of Carbs vs. Fats vs. Proteins in the garden.
    63|- **Trade/Barter Value:** Assigning "Scarcity Values" to specific high-density crops (e.g., Garlic, Potatoes).
    64|- **Off-Grid Recipe Integration:** Suggesting meals based on what is currently hitting "Harvest" status in the calendar.
    65|
    66|---
    67|
    68|## 6. Discussion Points for Stakeholders
    69|1. **Accuracy vs. Simplicity:** Should we use "Ideal Yields" or allow users to set a "Skill Level" modifier (Beginner/Expert)?
    70|2. **Privacy:** Since this data is highly sensitive for survivalists, should we explicitly state that all data remains in `localStorage` and is never sent to a server?
    71|3. **Hardware Integration:** Is there interest in manual "Tank Level" inputs for water, or should we strictly stick to "Projected" values from weather?
    72|", "total_lines": 71, "file_size": 3875, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

This is a really strong concept. Honestly, this is the kind of feature that could make Homesteader Labs a go-to tool rather than just a nice-looking site. Here are my thoughts:

========================================================================
REVIEW: CALORIC SECURITY & SURVIVAL INVENTORY STRATEGY
========================================================================

WHAT I LIKE:
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  The "Days of Autonomy" metric is brilliant. It takes all the
  separate tools you already have (planting calendar, weather station,
  survival index) and unifies them into a single number that actually
  means something to your user. "You have 47 days of water" is 10x
  more useful than raw catchment scores.

  The "Closed Loop" architecture makes a lot of sense. You already
  have the Production side (planting calendar with 22 crops, frost
  dates, succession planting) and the Environmental side (weather API,
  survival/planting indices). The missing piece is exactly what this
  doc describes -- connecting those to consumption needs.

  The integration with existing weather telemetry (Section 4) is
  where the real power is. Drought warnings that auto-reduce yield
  projections, frost alerts tied to actual inventory -- that turns
  this from a calculator into an actual early warning system.

  Privacy-first approach (discussion point #2) is exactly right for
  this audience. localStorage-only is a feature, not a limitation.
  I'd make it prominent in the UI: "ALL DATA STAYS ON YOUR DEVICE"

THINGS TO THINK ABOUT:
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  1. PHASE 1 IS UNDERSPECIFIED
     The doc says "... (rest of Phase 1)" -- this is actually the
     most important phase because you need a calories-per-crop data
     table. You already have 22 crops in crops.ts with planting
     timing, but they have no yield or calorie data. That's the
     foundation everything else builds on. I'd flesh this out first:

       interface CropNutrition {
         avgYieldPerPlant: number;   // lbs
         caloriesPer100g: number;
         storageLifeDays: number;    // shelf life post-harvest
         waterNeedsGallonsPerWeek: number;
       }

  2. SCOPE CREEP RISK
     Water catchment, solar, battery, food -- that's 4 systems. I'd
     recommend shipping Caloric Security (food) first since you already
     have the crop database. Water catchment second (simple formula).
     Solar/energy third. Each one is independently valuable.

  3. YIELD ESTIMATION IS HARD
     The formula (Plants * Yield * Calories) is clean on paper, but
     real yields swing wildly. The "Skill Level" modifier in your
     discussion points is a good start. I'd suggest:
       - Beginner: 50% of ideal yield
       - Intermediate: 75%
       - Expert: 90%
     You'll never hit 100% -- pests, weather, mistakes.

  4. THE UX QUESTION
     Where does this live? A new top-level route (/tools/autonomy/)
     or a tab within the weather station? Given the scope, I think
     it deserves its own page with the FieldStationLayout wrapper.
     It pulls from planting calendar data + weather + user config.

  5. HOUSEHOLD SIZE IS THE KEY INPUT
     Every formula divides by household size. This should be one of
     the first things you ask for in the config. 1 person vs 4 people
     is a 4x swing in days-of-autonomy.

MY TAKE ON THE DISCUSSION POINTS:
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  #1 Accuracy vs Simplicity:
     Both. Default to conservative "Beginner" estimates, let users
     override. Under-promising is better than over-promising when
     it's survival planning.

  #2 Privacy:
     Absolutely make this explicit. Add a badge or notice on the
     dashboard: "Zero telemetry. Your data never leaves this device."
     This audience will appreciate it and it's a competitive moat.

  #3 Hardware Integration:
     Start with manual inputs (current water level, pantry inventory).
     Manual data entry is totally fine for v1 -- these users are
     already tracking things on clipboards. Later you could add
     IoT/LoRa integration (you already sell Heltec V3 boards in the
     shop -- nice synergy there).

BOTTOM LINE:
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  Ship it in phases. Phase 1 = add calorie/yield data to crops.ts
  and build a basic "Days of Food" calculator connected to the
  planting calendar. That alone is a killer feature nobody else
  offers in this space. Water catchment is phase 2. Energy is
  phase 3.

  Want me to start building it?

Okay, here's the honest assessment:

========================================================================
CROPS.TS ARCHITECTURE REVIEW
========================================================================

WHAT YOU HAVE NOW:
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  - 22 crops, all vegetables, hardcoded in a single 459-line TS file
  - Good type system: Crop + Variety interfaces with planting timing,
    succession, lunar affinity, sun/spacing/notes
  - Category field exists ('vegetable' | 'herb' | 'fruit') but only
    'vegetable' is used
  - 4 helper functions (getById, getAll, getByCategory, getVarieties)
  - No yield, calorie, water needs, or preservation data
  - No concept of perennials (fruit trees, berry bushes)

THE QUESTION: Do you need a database?

SHORT ANSWER: Not yet. But the current single-file approach will break
down around 80-100 crops. Here's why:

========================================================================
THE SCALING PROBLEM
========================================================================

Your current crops.ts is ~460 lines for 22 crops (~21 lines/crop avg).
Here's what your vision adds:

  HERBS (cooking + medicinal + tea):
    Basil, cilantro, dill, parsley, rosemary, thyme, oregano, sage,
    mint, chamomile, lavender, echinacea, lemon balm, calendula,
    comfrey, yarrow, elderflower...
    ~20-30 crops

  FRUITS:
    Strawberries, blueberries, blackberries, raspberries, grapes,
    figs, apples, pears, peaches, plums, cherries, persimmons...
    ~15-20 crops

  Plus caloric security data per crop, preservation methods,
  water needs, companion planting, medicinal uses...

That's 60-70+ crops, and each one is getting more data fields.
At 30+ lines per crop (with the new fields), you're looking at
a 2000+ line file. That's maintainable but painful.

========================================================================
MY RECOMMENDATION: JSON + TYPES (not a database)
========================================================================

You don't need Postgres or SQLite. Your data is:
  - Read-only (users don't create crops)
  - Static per release (doesn't change at runtime)
  - Small enough to ship as a bundle (~50-100KB)
  - No relational queries needed

What I'd do:

  1. SPLIT CROPS INTO JSON DATA FILES BY CATEGORY

    content/crops/
      vegetables.json      # your existing 22
      herbs.json            # cooking, medicinal, tea
      fruits-annual.json    # strawberries, melons
      fruits-perennial.json # trees, bushes, vines

  2. EXTEND THE TYPE SYSTEM

    The current Crop interface is missing fields you'll need for
    herbs, fruits, perennials, and caloric security:

    interface Crop {
      // ...existing fields...

      // NEW: Growth habit (critical for fruits/perennials)
      growthHabit: 'annual' | 'biennial' | 'perennial';
      yearsToFirstHarvest?: number;  // 0 for annuals, 3-5 for trees

      // NEW: Caloric security
      yield: {
        avgPerPlant: number;     // lbs per plant per season
        unit: 'lbs' | 'oz' | 'bunches';
        caloriesPer100g: number;
        proteinPer100g?: number;
        storageLifeDays: number; // fresh shelf life
      };

      // NEW: Water needs (for autonomy calc)
      waterNeedsPerWeek: number;  // gallons per plant

      // NEW: Preservation
      preservation: {
        method: 'canning' | 'freezing' | 'drying' | 'fermentation'
                | 'root-cellar' | 'tincture' | 'infusion';
        shelfLifeMonths: number;
        notes: string;
      }[];

      // NEW: Use cases (herbs especially)
      uses?: ('cooking' | 'medicinal' | 'tea' | 'companion'
              | 'pollinator' | 'pest-deterrent' | 'dye')[];

      // NEW: Companion planting
      companions?: string[];     // crop IDs that grow well together
      antagonists?: string[];    // crop IDs to keep apart
    }

  3. A THIN LOADER LAYER (replaces the current hardcoded array)

    lib/tools/planting-calendar/cropLoader.ts

    - Imports the JSON files
    - Validates against the TypeScript types at build time
    - Provides the same API: getAllCrops(), getCropById(), etc.
    - Adds new queries: getCropsByUse('medicinal'),
      getCropsByPreservation('fermentation'),
      getCompanions('tomato')

  4. PERENNIAL HANDLING

    This is the biggest structural gap. Your current system assumes
    everything is plant-harvest-done within one season. Fruit trees
    and berry bushes need:

    - Multi-year timeline (plant year 1, first harvest year 3-5)
    - Pruning schedule (not just planting dates)
    - Chill hours requirement (like frost dates but for dormancy)
    - Yield curve (young tree vs mature tree)

    I'd add a separate PerennialSchedule type rather than trying
    to cram trees into the succession planting model.

========================================================================
WHY NOT A DATABASE?
========================================================================

  - No dynamic user-contributed content (yet)
  - No complex relational queries needed
  - JSON files are version-controlled, diffable, easy to edit
  - Zero infrastructure -- no server, no migrations, no connection
  - Works with static export / Vercel / any deployment
  - Users never write to it

  If you eventually want user-submitted crops or community data,
  THEN you'd want a database. But that's a different product phase.

========================================================================
THE "HOMESTEADER OS" ANGLE
========================================================================

You're right that this is evolving into something bigger. The pieces
are already there:

  Current:
    [Planting Calendar] + [Weather Station] + [Survival Index]
    + [Fabrication Workbench] + [Shop]

  With this expansion:
    [Crop Database]
      -> feeds [Planting Calendar]
      -> feeds [Caloric Security Dashboard]
      -> feeds [Preservation Planner]
      -> feeds [Water/Energy Autonomy Tracker]

  The crop data becomes the CORE DATA MODEL that everything else
  references. That's why getting the types right now matters more
  than the storage mechanism.

========================================================================
SUGGESTED BUILD ORDER
========================================================================

  1. Extend the Crop type with yield/preservation/uses/growthHabit
  2. Add caloric data to existing 22 vegetables
  3. Split into JSON files by category
  4. Add herbs (cooking first, medicinal second)
  5. Add annual fruits (strawberries, melons)
  6. Build the Caloric Security dashboard
  7. Add perennial fruits (trees/bushes) with multi-year model
  8. Build the Preservation Planner

Want me to start with step 1-3? I can extend the types, add the
caloric/preservation data to your existing 22 crops, and restructure
into JSON files without breaking any existing functionality.

```

Here's a quick reference for the fields and valid values:

```
REQUIRED FIELDS:
  id                  string          lowercase, hyphenated (e.g. "lemon-balm")
  name                string          display name (e.g. "Lemon Balm")
  category            "herb"          always "herb" for this file
  icon                string          emoji
  varieties           array           at least 1 variety object
  startIndoors        number | null   days before last frost (null if direct sow only)
  transplant          number | null   days after last frost to transplant
  directSow           number | null   days relative to last frost (negative = before)
  daysToMaturity      number          average days from planting to harvest
  successionEnabled   boolean         true for annuals, false for perennials
  successionInterval  number          weeks between plantings (0 if no succession)
  successionMax       number          max plantings per season (1 if no succession)
  sun                 "full" | "partial" | "shade"
  spacing             string          e.g. "8-12\" apart"
  notes               string[]        practical growing tips

OPTIONAL FIELDS (but please include all):
  lunarAffinity       "waxing" | "waning"     leaf herbs = waxing, root = waning
  growthHabit         "annual" | "biennial" | "perennial"
  yearsToFirstHarvest number          0 for most, 1-2 for woody herbs, 2-3 for elderberry
  waterNeedsPerWeek   number          gallons per plant (0.25-0.75 for most herbs)
  yield               object          see below
  preservation        array           see below
  uses                string[]        see valid values below
  companions          string[]        crop IDs from any JSON file
  antagonists         string[]        crop IDs to keep apart

YIELD OBJECT:
  avgPerPlant         number          oz or lbs per plant per season
  unit                "lbs" | "oz" | "bunches"
  caloriesPer100g     number          USDA value
  proteinPer100g      number
  carbsPer100g        number
  fatPer100g          number
  storageLifeDays     number          fresh shelf life

PRESERVATION ENTRY:
  method              one of: "canning", "freezing", "drying", "dehydrating",
                      "fermentation", "pickling", "tincture", "infusion",
                      "oil-preserving", "jam", "smoking", "salt-curing",
                      "root-cellar", "cold-storage"
  shelfLifeMonths     number
  notes               string

VALID USES:
  "cooking", "medicinal", "tea", "companion", "pollinator",
  "pest-deterrent", "dye", "fiber", "aromatherapy", "livestock-feed"

VARIETY OBJECT:
  id                  string          lowercase hyphenated
  name                string          display name
  daysToMaturity      number
  type                string          descriptive (e.g. "Sweet", "Medicinal")
  special             string[]        notable traits
```

```
